JavaScript Core Concepts (ES6 Edition): Notes From Will Huang's Kaohsiung Class
This post is mostly me jotting down a few of the more fun things I picked up from Will Huang's JavaScript class in Kaohsiung.
JavaScript in Practice: Core Concepts (ES6 Edition), Kaohsiung
First off, big thanks to my company for paying so I could go take Will Huang's "
Here's roughly what the class covered:
Objects, variables, and types in depth
The conceptual differences between var, let, and const
A detailed look at JS primitive types and object types
The root object concept (global vs local variables)
Really understanding function objects and how to use them
Default Parameters and how to apply them
Understanding Closures and their relationship to the Scope chain

The slides aren't allowed to be shared publicly, so I'm just going to write up my takeaways here. I won't explain some parts in full detail — mostly because I'd really recommend just going and taking Will's in-person class yourself.
Let's start with arrays. Normally you'd write a plain array like this:

That's the basic way to add to and write out an array. What about reading values? Besides the dot operator, you can also use square brackets. And here's a fun one — say you have an empty array, but you want to grab the 99th position and assign a value to it. What happens?
Click for the answer (think it through first)
The reason is that to let you put a value at index 99, JavaScript pre-fills a bunch of empty slots ahead of it.
Next up is something pretty fun — JavaScript obfuscation tools. There are plenty of them online, but at the end of the day it's still just JavaScript. For example, head over to

You'd never actually use this in real development, but it's a great way to show that JS really is bizarre — even something like this runs.
too. In JavaScript, when you declare a variable without assigning it a value, it defaults to
What happens if you assign a value and then re-declare it?
. The main reason is that JavaScript doesn't overwrite the existing value just because you re-declared it — unless you also re-assign a value at declaration time.
JavaScript itself is a pointer concept
For this one I'd really lean toward just taking Will's class, so I'm not going to spoil it~
Primitive types can't be extended or hold properties
Primitive types can't have properties freely added to them, so they're not allowed to hold properties.
But because a primitive type gets temporarily wrapped in a wrapper object, it inherits the methods from that wrapper object.
Even going through the wrapper object, though, you still can't modify the primitive's properties.
Number is an incredibly evil, terrifying thing. Basically, the moment you write
on it, it's still a number type.

Number('100a'); // NaN
typeof Number('100a'); // is number ??????
has its own gotchas too. For instance, whatever you pass in, if part of it can't be converted to a number, that part just gets ignored:
parseInt('100,000'); // 100, because the comma is a string.
// 100, because the comma is a string.
Also, when doing calculations in JavaScript, never do math with decimals. JavaScript uses a floating-point system, so calculations involving decimals can be off by a surprising amount. For example:
. The name literally means "not defined," but it's a primitive type
an object, and also a variable — which is why
is actually valid. And back in IE8, you could even re-assign it to a different object.

The above is just a rough list of some of the fun bits — for the real thing I'd still recommend taking the class. That said, I will absolutely not admit that at one point I kept wanting to fall asleep. And during the class, Will genuinely calls on everyone to answer questions, even getting people up to draw on the board, using all sorts of methods to make you explain the concepts. And when you

explain something, Will follows up with even more questions to test you (huh?!) — mainly to make sure you've actually absorbed the concept and can really explain it. So personally I think it's a class that's well worth it, the only downside being that your head really hurts by the end XD.

A few of the more fun things I picked up from Will Huang's "JavaScript in Practice: Core Concepts (ES6 Edition)" class in Kaohsiung. Empty arrays that fill themselves with blanks, var that won't get overwritten, why Number is pure evil, and a handful of other JS quirks worth knowing.
